result:
I’ve been thinking about the long hallway that runs through memory, the one I always enter from the middle, never the ends. It starts just behind my left ear, a low hum that becomes a knocking in syllables, and suddenly the door is there, unpainted, slightly warped as if someone once leaned against it too long while crying. I open it, step in, and the walls lengthen on either side like sentences trying to contain too many clauses. To the left is yesterday: dishes still dripping beside the sink, the silver whistle of a kettle caught mid-wail, the cat’s tail uncoiling from a patch of sun. To the right is the stretch labeled “so long ago it might be someone else’s life,” though each frame is edged with my fingerprints, raised scar-tissue memories I can read like Braille. I have promised myself to stay upright, to keep my pace light and straightforward, but I keep veering off into side rooms, drawn by the clink of ice in apossibly) my grandfather’s lemonade. The air behind every door is thicker: each breath feels like I’m drinking history through cloth.

The hallway is well lit by a skylight I never installed. It looks like the one I stared through the summer I learned to float, age eight or nine, a neighborhood pool smelling of chlorine and grilled hot dogs. Through the glass I can see the undersides of clouds moving west, the same direction the heat always went in my childhood town. From somewhere back there I hear the slap of bare feet on hot pavement, the flick of playing cards clothespinned to bicycle spokes so they roar like angry bees. I don’t know whether I am hearing a recording or whether time is being performed live by some tireless cast of children. I clutch a flashlight in my right pocket because the hallway knows outages, the way a story knows the page can end.

If I keep walking left—counterclockwise along the corona of now—I reach the refrigerator in the house I left at twenty-two. It contains a single jar of green curry paste and a letter I wrote to my future self about renouncing all possessions except music. I open the letter, reread the part where “every surface will be a drum.” I smile the way one smiles at a younger sibling who has declared herself Queen of the World. Then the refrigerator light stutters; its bulb is a dying star. I close the door before the darkness reaches all the way in. Someone told me when I was small that turning the fridge light on and off too fast would break it, and I still act as if appliances can die of hurt feelings.

Farther along, I find the table where the physics students gathered at 2 a.m., debating Everett and Schrödinger over instant ramen. Half-empty bottles, red plastic cups, a stack of quizzes no one ever collected. I keep expecting to see the chalk equations breathing, expanding, like yeasted dough. Instead the numbers lie there quiet, resistant to narrative, daring me to recall the exact derivation for the probability density of a particle in a box. I’m not sure whether I learned the equation or dreamed it. One semester I slept so little I began writing problem sets in the margins of a novel about Antarctic explorers, which seemed perfectly rational because a wave function, too, is an explorer, trekking across the probability tundra.

Even farther left is the morning the Berlin Wall fell, televised on a Zenith set layered with dust. I am five, sitting on my father’s lap. He smells of coffee and the peppermints he chewed so my mother wouldn’t know he smoked. The television pours gray crowds into our living room: sledgehammers raised, chunks of painted concrete sailing into history’s discard pile. I don’t understand why the people cry and laugh at the same time. My father says, “Remember this, it’s important,” though his eyes already know how soon I’ll forget, the way an orchard forgets each individual apple once the harvest is over. Now I stand in front of that scene, older than he was then, and I want to yell back across the decades, “Turn the camera! Show him I kept my promise. I remembered.” But the figures only flicker, magnetic and unreachable.

There is a thermodynamics to grief: the colder the loss, the slower the molecule of remembrance moves, until the image is suspended like a snowflake under glass. I keep the hallway cool. Sometimes I press my warm hand against a frozen pane and watch the frost retreat, forming a palm-shaped rivulet of clarity. My mother’s laugh arrives that way: suddenly fat tears wobble along the edge of my vision, and then I hear the time she imitated the turkey gobble at Thanksgiving, stuffing the dishtowel under her chin like a wattle, everyone at the table losing composure at once. I laugh with them in the now, but like a seashell the sound is hollowed by distance.

I turn right, the direction we call “long ago.” Here the hallway narrows until I must walk sideways, a crab shimmying through my own history. The first room belongs to the old maple tree whose lowest branch I could reach only by standing on the bike rack. That summer I believed if I climbed high enough I’d scrape the blue enamel of sky and find stenciled beneath it: MADE BY YOU-KNOW-WHO. I spent whole afternoons aloft with a notebook, writing observations: June 4—cloud shaped like otter. June 5—wind from my grandmother’s direction. On the day the branch cracked and pitched me onto the lawn (bruised coccyx, miraculous), Twin Towers were still standing in the immovable tomorrow we took for granted. The maple room smells of sap, green and metallic like the first smear of blood from a scraped knee. I lick my wrist impersonally, searching for the childish salt, but the skin is only adult, impatient, already leaving.

Farther right is the first story I ever told a friend, age seven, about a pigeon named Fetch who carried countries instead of letters. I placed the tiny atlas pages like postage stamps on his left wing. Under the maple’s shade I narrated it while she listened solemnly, braid waggling like a metronome. At the climax Fetch delivers a miniature France to a mouse who ran out of wine. My friend laughed so hard a grasshopper leapt out of the lawn and startled us both. In the hallway I find the page where the tale broke off mid-sentence: “And then the pigeon realized the next envelope was—” The rest missing, forever blank. I still wonder what it was. A passport? A war declaration? An apology from someone? Stories dry in fragments. They crumble like bread too long in the toaster. But that’s their beauty: every burned edge a shore for someone else to build a new bridge across.

Past that lies the elementary school auditorium, erected in 1912, smelling of cedar shavings and tempera paint. I had painted a sun with fists, orange and violent, few rays, like it had walked into a bar fight. My teacher knelt to meet my eyes and said, “That’s not how you draw a sun.” She turned my paper over, gave me a fresh sheet, demonstrated a pale-yellow circle with sky. Thirty years later I stand inside the hallway and and speak aloud, correcting the record: “My sun knew what it was about. It had reasons.” My voice rebounds off glossy wood, then disappears like chalk dust into the rafters where someone once left an ancient basketball deflating slowly, year by year, sighing.

I understand now: the hallway is not strictly chronological—Nor is it metric, nor merciful, nor mine alone. Other walkers pass me. A girl with hennaed hands (“Hello, stranger,” she nods—was she in that elevator in Marrakesh?). An elderly man riveted to the spot where I learned multiplication tables (he looks up, distracted, and recites them: six times seven is forty-two, forty-two, forty-two.) Each person hums their own walking song. Their footfalls overlap, competing frequencies. Sometimes I catch word-fragments: —gravity— —cranberry— —undress the telescope— I don’t know the rules governing contact. Once I tried to speak to the woman pacing with a blue umbrella indoors (storm the year I was eleven). She responded by opening it, producing the scent of wet wool and the flicker of October lightning. By the time I blinked she was gone, umbrella collapsing into a baton of overcast sky. I wonder who among these walkers is future, who is past. I wonder whether I myself am trespasser, haunting another mind’s architecture.

In order not to get irrevocably lost, I developed a mantra: Count rooms where music happened. I tally: the bedroom where mix tapes unspooled into empty nights; the rental car where Hamilton blasted in Joshua Tree; the Chicago club bathroom where a stranger taught me the ocarina riff from a Zelda game; the hospital corridor where a nurse hummed “Blackbird” while changing IV bags. Each snippet attaches like luggage label to the rolling cart of selfhood. Music transgresses sealed hours, sutures days into illusion of continuum. The hallway likes that. It plays along, broadcasting snatches—sometimes tinny, sometimes cathedral loud—so passages not meant to align suddenly synchronize. For an instant the kitchen where I burned rice at nineteen syncopates with the Bangkok apartment where the street band played a xylophone made of beer bottles. I smell jasmine and scorched grain simultaneously; neurons fuse momentarily braid.

Today, though, the music shelves are bare. I hear instead the unlovely grind of a vacuum cleaner pushing dirt into someone else’s Saturday morning. The hallway contracts like metal under frost. My palms prick with cold sweat. I know this sensation: the part of me that edits fear into curiosity has logged off, leaving only raw pupil. Memory is unstable when the appliances revolt. I “check” my pocket for the flashlight though I know it will not flicker when summoned. I breathe through the panic by reminding myself everyone’s hallway has its brownouts. Some nights are just janitors mopping floorboards with electroshock of regret. I keep moving. Left, then right again, looping like a cursive e.

Eventually I arrive at the door that is noticeably warmer. The handle is sticky with melted sugar, remnants of funnel cake. A carnival memory. Late teens, county fair, ferris wheel candescent against August sky, my date leaning into my shoulder, neither of us certain whether the moment should be labeled romance or convenient geometry. We argued over which constellation hovered above the wheel. He said Orion; I said Cassiopeia because I liked the myth—queen upside down half the year as punishment for vanity. I realize now we were both utterly wrong; late August, neither constellation rose until after midnight. But what remains is not accuracy but the willingness to embroider the night with names. I open the door.

Inside, the carnival has gone feral. Striped canvas roofs sag; popcorn vines strangle prizes; the carousel horses have freed themselves and wander, ceramic eyes reflecting my older form. One mare approaches. chips of turquoise paint flake from her flanks. She bows slightly, nudges my sternum as if asking for direction. I place my palm against the cold wood between her ears. “Hey, old girl,” I whisper, unsure who I am addressing. “How long does a wonder last?” She doesn’t answer, but lifts her muzzle skyward. I look up. The ferris wheel is rusted immobile, forming a ferric orrery against cloudless dark. Sparks of light are not stars but carousel bulbs burnt out one by one—only the ghosts of their glow remain like afterimages in retinal fatigue. The fair once turned but the axle of childhoodibility froze the moment we disembarked and thought life started somewhere else.

Next stall over, the ring toss game is attended by a goldfish in a plastic bag suspended on a hook. His mouth opens, closes, words bubbling up. I lean in until our foreheads nearly touch plastic. “Tell me,” he says in a voice multitudinous, the childhood ensemble of all carnival goldfish ever won and lost, “Did you ever do the thing you told yourself you’d do?” The sky tilts. For a second I feel my self-assurance waterfall out of my shoes. Of course not. Of course yes. There is a list I wrote on a napkin, fifteen ambitions—play cello beneath bridge, send postcard to stranger, read Proust, apologize properly. I tick some off: played viola, not cello, beneath an underpass in Edinburgh once for coins. Sent anonymous birthday card to ex-roommate from fake New Zealand address. Read three swannsworth of Proust and stopped to breathe. That counts as partial absolution. Still, the fish’s gaze remains steady. Perhaps he isn’t demanding completion—he wants only acknowledgement. My apologies glow softer under carnival string lights.

I step back into the hallway, smelling of powdered sugar and burnt filament. Ahead, a new room has grafted itself overnight, a phenomenon that happens when I dream in languages I haven’t studied. The doorframe is painted institutional green—hospital, I recognize. Inside, a waiting room chairs of orange vinyl crouch in agony. On one sits my mother’s coat, moss-green wool with brass buttons the color of hindsight. The coat is alone, collar up, as though hailing a taxi. I approach gently, half-expecting the fabric to speak. It doesn’t, but pockets bulge with letters addressed to 2030. I pull one out, find my own handwriting:

“Dear Future, When you read this you might be vacuuming Saturn’s rings or watching meteor from porch swing. Either is fine. I write from a day when father can still speak; he just told a nurse the secret to perfect pie crust is vodka instead of water. Mother laughed so hard she hiccupped, a sound science will one day measure as joy’s frequency. Capture that hiccup in a jar. Keep it on your desk against the future’s disappointments.”

The letter ends abruptly in mid-sentence, as if the pen refused to cap hope. I open ten more—each repeats, verbatim, until the stack becomes a Bach canon at the octave. Same phrases, same position of inkblot shaped like sleeping cat. The repetition comforts me more than novelty would. Somewhere in singularity that hiccup still echoes continuous re-sound, immune to laws against perpetual motion. I pat the coat’s sleeve, leave the letters nestled where they were. Coat does not object; coats are patient creatures.

Beyond this corridor curved like a question mark lies the coast shared by every vacation. The sand is made from billions of ground-down clocks. I remove shoes, relish the warm ticking between toes. The ocean sound is the same waveform whether produced by electrons on noise machine or Atlantic hurling itself at granite, which proves that everything is both counterfeit and genuine depending on calibration of attention. My childhood shell collection lies half buried. I kneel, uncover the conch whose spiral if held to ear roared not sea but late-night jazz radio: a tinny sax reproaching me for misplacing the sheet music labeled, simply, Solo. Once at that nighttime shore I promised to practice trumpet every dawn; instead I overslept and the instrument grew dust like gray fur on a dying animal. I forgive myself again under conch-blown hush of passages. The tide erases footprints instantly; this reassures me artfully that erasements are natural.

The hallway now resembles both library and tide pool—text spines glisten, starfish grip shelves. I reach for one volume labeled “Almost Kisses.” The first page smells of electric lights in subway car, 4 a.m., 2005: strangers’ heads loll like sunflower heavy with dusk. Then the woman beside me half wakes; nods at map in my lap; our fingers accidentally align as we trace route downtown. Record skips—moment stretches—then stops short. We never speak, never exchange names. The page ends mid-sentence with ellipsis in parentheses, implying an alternate universe still rolling where mouths met, where daily commutes braided. I shelve it gently next to “Recipes Abandoned Mid-Spice,” beside “Freckles Found on Other People’s Shoulders.”

Far across, I spot the room that stays locked most days. Around knob, someone (myself? future custodian?) tied red thread like threading artery. I hesitate. Entry protocol demands I leave one memory at threshold. I consider offerings, reject them: turtle-shaped eraser would demean the gravity, rock concert ticket stub too flimsy. Into pocket I find one lint-soft threadbare pebble—worry stone inherited from Uncle Ben who kept it during war and returned missing an ear but carrying luck. Seems appropriate. I set stone on floor outside, take a breath. Red thread dissolves like sugar in rain. Door opens.

Inside is absolute quiet, the kind invented when universe was smaller, quieter. Walls white as unsent emails. Centerpiece: a single steaming cup of coffee sits on low table. No chair accompanies it; implies I ought to kneel. Steam curls, shapes in cursive the name of every atom inside me. I touch cup—ceramic exactly body temperature. Liquid mirrors ceiling’s fluorescent calm. One sip would surely taste of everything unsaid between everyone. But I do not drink. Someday I might; for now silence is enough. I back away slowly, rethread door’s red slip, pocket returning worry stone, perhaps slightly heavier, perhaps not. Doors, I’ve learned, appreciate warning before departure.

Returning the way I came demands retracing steps that have rearranged while I paced. Hallway demonstrates Möbius behavior. Left is sometimes right; right sometimes adjacent to maple tree once again. Chestnuts scatter across floor like marbles drunk on gravity. The hum behind ear returns, deeper, like generator beneath city. I know spiral nears end when one begins recognizing doors not yet opened as things one has merely forgotten. Overhead, the artificial skylight dims to lavender dusk. Somewhere carnival horses graze among bumper cars; somewhere father’s coffee grows lukewarm. Each step resonates exponentially—soon ceiling will lower and metamorphose into bedroom of present night, mattress waiting with sheets already annotated by dreams.

But before complete compaction, I allow myself to construct forward. Not prediction—just adjacent potential. I visualize a room that has not earned space yet: small, round, flooded with paned amber light like honeycomb. The floor is checkered with tiles from Lisbon sidewalks, black basalt and white limestone tess tessellation wave. On waist-high pedestal sits a metronome ticking neither too fast nor slow, calibrated exactly to heart rate of planet. Beside it, three saplings in clay pots—minor chords become living wood. One sprouts leaves like sheetmusic, staffs laden with green-note berries. One moves in wind that arrives from hallway’s left; another silent but reflects whatever moon has wandered. In this room I will sit one day, pen angled to catch illumination. I will write a letter back to today’s version, telling details of how worry stone earned scarlet embroidery and why it no longer needs to be clenched between fingers. I will also mention the outcome of the unfinished pigeon story. (Fetch, it turns out, wrote back to the mouse: Dear friend, your miniature France has taught me geography of longing. During flight I glimpsed every geography squeezed between trying and arriving. Consider the envelope my atlas. P.S. Your wine ran out but the story distilled.) The room may truly exist by the time breath catches up; perhaps we share boundaries, interlibrary lending system of solitudes.

For now, I exit the hallway via the same small unpainted door through which I entered. I step backward one deliberate pace—rules of polite egress—then gently close. As latch snaps, dimensions equalize and I find myself standing in my actual body: same coffee shop where I began this journey, half-drunk cappuccino cold, barista now sweeping crumbs. Someone’s earbuds leak piano chord progress that matches hallway’s metronome. I inhale, smelling creosote and cinnamon. My tongue translates, automatically, into remembered flavors of entire lifetimes ingested and still digesting. The world outside window continues as if unpaused—a bus bellowing, a mother pinning toddler mittens. And yet the unpainted door glints for an instant in reflected fluorescent, like an after-image on closed eyelids.

I understand: any room can be reopened simply by closing the right book at the wrong page. Memory craves audience participation. So I pocket small pencil found beneath chair, decide to leave margin for amendments. The 2500th word arrives humming.

The letter ends abruptly in mid-sentence, as if the pen refused to cap hope. I open ten more—each repeats, verbatim, until the stack becomes a Bach canon at the octave. Same phrases, same position of inkblot shaped like sleeping cat. The repetition comforts me more than novelty would. Somewhere in singularity that hiccup still echoes continuous re-sound, immune to laws against perpetual motion. I pat the coat’s sleeve, leave the letters nestled where they were. Coat does not object; coats are patient creatures.

Beyond this corridor curved like a question mark lies the coast shared by every vacation. The sand is made from billions of ground-down clocks. I remove shoes, relish the warm ticking between toes. The ocean sound is the same waveform whether produced by electrons on noise machine or Atlantic hurling itself at granite, which proves that everything is both counterfeit and genuine depending on calibration of attention. My childhood shell collection lies half buried. I kneel, uncover the conch whose spiral if held to ear roared not sea but late-night jazz radio: a tinny sax reproaching me for misplacing the sheet music labeled, simply, Solo. Once at that nighttime shore I promised to practice trumpet every dawn; instead I overslept and the instrument grew dust like gray fur on a dying animal. I forgive myself again under conch-blown hush of passages. The tide erases footprints instantly; this reassures me artfully that erasements are natural.

The hallway now resembles both library and tide pool—text spines glisten, starfish grip shelves. I reach for one volume labeled “Almost Kisses.” The first page smells of electric lights in subway car, 4 a.m., 2005: strangers’ heads loll like sunflower heavy with dusk. Then the woman beside me half wakes; nods at map in my lap; our fingers accidentally align as we trace route downtown. Record skips—moment stretches—then stops short. We never speak, never exchange names. The page ends mid-sentence with ellipsis in parentheses, implying an alternate universe still rolling where mouths met, where daily commutes braided. I shelve it gently next to “Recipes Abandoned Mid-Spice,” beside “Freckles Found on Other People’s Shoulders.”

Far across, I spot the room that stays locked most days. Around knob, someone (myself? future custodian?) tied red thread like threading artery. I hesitate. Entry protocol demands I leave one memory at threshold. I consider offerings, reject them: turtle-shaped eraser would demean the gravity, rock concert ticket stub too flimsy. Into pocket I find one lint-soft threadbare pebble—worry stone inherited from Uncle Ben who kept it during war and returned missing an ear but carrying luck. Seems appropriate. I set stone on floor outside, take a breath. Red thread dissolves like sugar in rain. Door opens.

Inside is absolute quiet, the kind invented when universe was smaller, quieter. Walls white as unsent emails. Centerpiece: a single steaming cup of coffee sits on low table. No chair accompanies it; implies I ought to kneel. Steam curls, shapes in cursive the name of every atom inside me. I touch cup—ceramic exactly body temperature. Liquid mirrors ceiling’s fluorescent calm. One sip would surely taste of everything unsaid between everyone. But I do not drink. Someday I might; for now silence is enough. I back away slowly, rethread door’s red slip, pocket returning worry stone, perhaps slightly heavier, perhaps not. Doors, I’ve learned, appreciate warning before departure.

Returning the way I came demands retracing steps that have rearranged while I paced. Hallway demonstrates Möbius behavior. Left is sometimes right; right sometimes adjacent to maple tree once again. Chestnuts scatter across floor like marbles drunk on gravity. The hum behind ear returns, deeper, like generator beneath city. I know spiral nears end when one begins recognizing doors not yet opened as things one has merely forgotten. Overhead, the artificial skylight dims to lavender dusk. Somewhere carnival horses graze among bumper cars; somewhere father’s coffee grows lukewarm. Each step resonates exponentially—soon ceiling will lower and metamorphose into bedroom of present night, mattress waiting with sheets already annotated by dreams.

But before complete compaction, I allow myself to construct forward. Not prediction—just adjacent potential. I visualize a room that has not earned space yet: small, round, flooded with paned amber light like honeycomb. The floor is checkered with tiles from Lisbon sidewalks, black basalt and white limestone tess tessellation wave. On waist-high pedestal sits a metronome ticking neither too fast nor slow, calibrated exactly to heart rate of planet. Beside it, three saplings in clay pots—minor chords become living wood. One sprouts leaves like sheetmusic, staffs laden with green-note berries. One moves in wind that arrives from hallway’s left; another silent but reflects whatever moon has wandered. In this room I will sit one day, pen angled to catch illumination. I will write a letter back to today’s version, telling details of how worry stone earned scarlet embroidery and why it no longer needs to be clenched between fingers. I will also mention the outcome of the unfinished pigeon story. (Fetch, it turns out, wrote back to the mouse: Dear friend, your miniature France has taught me geography of longing. During flight I glimpsed every geography squeezed between trying and arriving. Consider the envelope my atlas. P.S. Your wine ran out but the story distilled.) The room may truly exist by the time breath catches up; perhaps we share boundaries, interlibrary lending system of solitudes.

For now, I exit the hallway via the same small unpainted door through which I entered. I step backward one deliberate pace—rules of polite egress—then gently close. As latch snaps, dimensions equalize and I find myself standing in my actual body: same coffee shop where I began this journey, half-drunk cappuccino cold, barista now sweeping crumbs. Someone’s earbuds leak piano chord progress that matches hallway’s metronome. I inhale, smelling creosote and cinnamon. My tongue translates, automatically, into remembered flavors of entire lifetimes ingested and still digesting. The world outside window continues as if unpaused—a bus bellowing, a mother pinning toddler mittens. And yet the unpainted door glints for an instant in reflected fluorescent, like an after-image on closed eyelids.

I understand: any room can be reopened simply by closing the right book at the wrong page. Memory craves audience participation. So I pocket small pencil found beneath chair, decide to leave margin for amendments. The 2500th word arrives humming.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
